text: What's one conversation from this week that earned this number? Name it — where you were, what made it easy.
closing_text: Good talk isn't luck. You know what it looks like now.

text: What did {partner} do lately that made talking feel easy? Name the thing, so they know to do it again.
closing_text: Now it has a name. Easier to repeat.

text: When talking works this well between you, there's usually an ingredient — timing, place, no phones. What's yours?
closing_text: Worth keeping within reach for a harder week.

text: Talking has felt harder than usual. From where you sit, what does the hard part look like? Just describe it — no fixing yet. You can skip this one. It'll keep.
What's one small thing that might make talking easier this week — under ten minutes? Offer it, don't assign it.
closing_text: That's enough for now. This topic will come back around.

text: Somewhere along the way, words between you got heavier. When did you first notice? Just the moment, from your side. If today isn't the day, skip it — it'll keep.
Pick one ten-minute window this week to talk about something that isn't logistics. When would yours be?
closing_text: Small windows count. That's enough for today.

text: When talking stalls, each person is usually protecting something. What might you be protecting? Describe it, don't solve it. Skipping is allowed.
What's one sentence you've been sitting on? Write it here — saying it out loud can come later.
closing_text: Writing it down is already a step. The rest can wait.

text: Your numbers landed far apart. What might {partner} be seeing about how you two talk that you might be missing?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: Think of a recent conversation {partner} might be carrying differently than you. What might it have felt like from their side?
closing_text: Two people can leave the same conversation with two different weights. Both are real.

text: What made this number high — a moment, a stretch of days, a feeling? Name it plainly.
closing_text: You know what close looks like for you two. Worth remembering.

text: What did {partner} do recently that helped you feel close? Small counts. Tell them it landed.
closing_text: Things that land deserve to be known.

text: Closeness like this usually has conditions — rest, time, privacy, ease. Which were present lately?
closing_text: Good conditions can be rebuilt on purpose. Now you know the recipe.

text: This one is tender, so go gently. From your side, what does the distance feel like — not why, just what? You can skip this one. It'll keep.
What's one small, low-stakes way to be close this week — a longer hug, sitting nearer, lights out at the same time? Offer one.
closing_text: Small and doable beats big and postponed. That's enough for now.

text: Distance rarely arrives all at once. When do you last remember closeness feeling easy? Just the memory, from where you sit. Skipping is fine — this will keep.
Name one thing that would make closeness feel a little more possible this week. Under ten minutes, nothing dramatic.
closing_text: A little more possible is how it starts. This will come back around.

text: Sometimes distance is tiredness, timing, or something unsaid. Without solving anything, what sits between you right now, from your side? Skip if today's not the day.
What's one gentle step toward each other this week — yours to offer, not theirs to owe?
closing_text: Offered, not owed. That's enough for now.

text: Your numbers are far apart here. What might {partner} be noticing about closeness lately that you might not be?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: Closeness gets felt differently by different people in different weeks. What might this stretch have felt like from {partner}'s side?
closing_text: A gap in numbers is usually a gap in vantage, not in wanting.

text: What's one small gesture from this week that made your number high? Name the actual moment.
closing_text: Small things, noticed, tend to multiply.

text: What does {partner} do that reads as love to you, even when it's tiny? Tell them it counts.
closing_text: Now they know it counts. That's how it keeps happening.

text: Warmth like this usually lives in routines — greetings, goodnights, passing touches. Which routine is carrying it?
closing_text: Protect the routine and the warmth protects itself.

text: Affection has felt thin lately. From your side, what does that look like day to day? Describe it, don't fix it yet. Skip if you'd rather — it'll keep.
What's one small act of warmth you could add this week — a real hello, a hand on a shoulder?
closing_text: Warmth comes back in small doses. That's enough for now.

text: When affection fades it's usually crowded out, not withdrawn. What's been crowding it, from where you sit? Just describe. You can skip this one.
Pick one moment in your day — leaving, returning, going to sleep — and add one warm gesture this week. Which moment?
closing_text: One warm moment a day is a quiet fortune. That's enough.

text: You each read affection in your own language. Describe what you've been missing, in yours — without a verdict on anyone. Skipping is fine.
Name one gesture in {partner}'s language, rather than your own, that you could try once this week.
closing_text: Their language, spoken once, says a lot. That's enough for now.

text: One of you is feeling the warmth more than the other right now. What might {partner} be counting that you're not?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: Affection can be given in one language and looked for in another. What language might {partner} be looking in?
closing_text: Different numbers here usually mean different languages, not different love.

text: Money felt steady to both of you. What's actually working — a habit, an agreement, a regular check-in? Name it.
closing_text: Systems that work deserve to be noticed while they're working.

text: What has {partner} done with money lately that you respect — earned, planned, held back, spoke up? Say so.
closing_text: Respect, said out loud, is rare around money. Worth repeating.

text: When money feels this calm, something is being done regularly. What's the routine you'd protect?
closing_text: Calm around money is built, not found. You built this.

text: Money came in low, and money is rarely just money. From your side, what's the uncomfortable part? Describe it without solving it. You can skip this one. It'll keep.
One small money step this week, under ten minutes — checking one balance, naming one worry out loud. What's yours?
closing_text: Ten minutes is plenty. This will come back around.

text: Behind most money stress there's a quieter fear. Without fixing anything, what's the fear from where you sit? Skip if it's too much today — it'll keep.
Name one number you'd both like to be able to say out loud without flinching. Just looking at it together is the whole step.
closing_text: Looking is enough for now. Deciding can wait.

text: You may be running different money stories — how you grew up, what safety costs. Briefly describe yours. No verdicts. It's fine to skip.
What's one small agreement you could try for a single week — one purchase check-in, one shared note? Propose it lightly.
closing_text: One week is a trial, not a contract. That's enough for now.

text: You're reading the money picture very differently. What might {partner} be looking at — a bill, a balance, a memory — that you're not?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: Money weighs differently on different shoulders. What might feel heavy, or light, from {partner}'s side right now?
closing_text: A gap here usually means two different histories, not two different levels of care.

text: Family felt good to both of you. What went well — a visit, a boundary, a moment of backup? Name it.
closing_text: Worth knowing what well looks like, for next time.

text: How did {partner} show up around family lately in a way you appreciated? Tell them what you saw.
closing_text: Being seen handling family is its own kind of thanks.

text: When family runs this smoothly, there's usually quiet coordination underneath. What's the move worth repeating?
closing_text: Keep the move. It clearly works.

text: Family came in low, and family is complicated for almost everyone. From your side, what's the hard part right now? Describe, don't fix. You can skip this — it'll keep.
One small thing this week — a text sent, a boundary named, a visit adjusted. What's your small thing?
closing_text: Small moves with family go further than big speeches. That's enough for now.

text: Sometimes the hard part isn't the family — it's feeling alone in handling them. Where do you feel solo in this, from your side? Skipping is allowed.
Name one moment coming up where you'd like backup, and what backup would look like. Keep it small.
closing_text: Knowing what backup looks like is most of getting it. That's enough for now.

text: Your families raised two different people with two different rulebooks. Which unspoken rule from yours might be in play here? Just notice it. Skip if you'd rather.
Propose one small adjustment for the next family moment — who answers, who drives, when you leave. One thing.
closing_text: One adjustment at a time. This will come back around.

text: You see the family picture differently right now. What might {partner} be carrying — an obligation, an old dynamic — that's less visible to you?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: It's easiest to see your own family clearly. What might your family look like from {partner}'s seat at the table?
closing_text: Two vantage points, one table. Both views are real.

text: Friendship felt healthy from both sides. What made the number high — a good night, easy room for separate plans?
closing_text: A social life that fits is worth noticing while it fits.

text: How has {partner} made room for your friendships lately? Name it — that kind of generosity is easy to miss.
closing_text: Generosity noticed tends to continue.

text: When social life works, a balance is being kept — together, apart, shared. What's the balance you'd keep?
closing_text: Now the balance has words. Easier to hold.

text: Friends came in low. From where you sit, what's off — too little time, the wrong mix, feeling left out? Describe it plainly. Skip if you'd rather — it'll keep.
One small social step this week — a text to an old friend, a plan made, an evening freed. What's yours?
closing_text: Friendships restart smaller than you'd think. That's enough for now.

text: Couples can lose friendships slowly without anyone choosing it. What have you missed lately, from your side? No blame in it — just the missing. You can skip this one.
Name one friendship — yours, theirs, or shared — worth ten minutes of tending this week, and what you'd do.
closing_text: Ten minutes of tending is real tending. This will come back around.

text: Feeling crowded and feeling left out can happen in the same house. Which is closer to yours right now? Describe it, don't defend it. Skipping is fine.
Suggest one plan for the next two weeks — a night with your people, a night with theirs, or one with both.
closing_text: One plan on the calendar is enough for now.

text: You're reading your social life differently. What might {partner} be wanting more of — or less of — that they haven't said?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: What do friendships give {partner} that might be hard for them to ask for out loud?
closing_text: Different numbers here often mean different needs for elsewhere, not less need for each other.

text: You both landed high on fun. Name the moment from this week you'd most want to happen again.
closing_text: Fun leaves clues. You just found one.

text: What did {partner} do this week that made you laugh? Tell them — laughter you name tends to come back.
closing_text: Now it's on the record. That's how running jokes start.

text: Good fun usually has a setting — a time of day, a place, no plans. What were the conditions?
closing_text: Now you have the recipe. Keep it somewhere handy.

text: Fun came in low, which usually means life got heavy — not that you got boring. From your side, what's been squeezing it out? Just describe. You can skip this — it'll keep.
One small dose of fun this week — ten minutes, zero planning. A game, a walk, a ridiculous video. What's your offer?
closing_text: Fun doesn't need a plan. That's enough for now.

text: When did you two last laugh together properly? Describe that moment from your side — what allowed it. No need to recreate it yet. Skipping is fine.
Pick one ordinary moment this week — a commute, a chore, dinner — and make it slightly more ridiculous. Which one?
closing_text: Ordinary plus slightly ridiculous is the whole formula. That's enough.

text: Sometimes one of you carries the fun and quietly gets tired. From where you sit, who's been carrying it lately? Describe gently. It's fine to skip — this will keep.
Name one low-effort thing you'd genuinely enjoy doing together this week. Small counts double.
closing_text: That's enough for now. Fun comes back around faster than most things.

text: You landed far apart on fun. What might count as fun for {partner} that you might be overlooking?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: One of you may be laughing more than the other lately. What might this stretch have felt like from {partner}'s side?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different weeks, not different senses of humor.

text: The future feels good from both sides. What's the shared picture that earned the number? Sketch it in a sentence.
closing_text: A future you can say in a sentence is one you can steer by.

text: What has {partner} done lately that made the future feel more possible — a plan, a saved dollar, a dream said out loud?
closing_text: Possibility is usually somebody's quiet doing. You just named whose.

text: When you talk about the future well, it tends to happen somewhere — long drives, late nights. Where does it happen for you?
closing_text: Protect the place and the talks will keep coming.

text: The future came in low, which can feel bigger than one day's answer. From your side, what feels foggy or stuck? Describe it — no plans required yet. You can skip this one. It'll keep.
One ten-minute future conversation this week — not the whole future, just one corner. Which corner would you pick?
closing_text: One corner at a time is how whole pictures get painted. That's enough for now.

text: Sometimes the future feels hard because two timelines quietly disagree. From your side, what timeline are you on? Just name it. Skipping is allowed.
Name one small thing you could do this week that your future selves would be glad about. Tiny counts.
closing_text: Tiny and done beats grand and discussed. This will come back around.

text: A deferred dream can sit quietly in a relationship for years. Is one of yours sitting there? Describe it gently — nobody has to act on it today. You can skip this.
What's one question about the future you'd like to ask each other this week? Just write the question.
closing_text: A good question is a step. The answer can take its time.

text: You see the road ahead differently right now. What might {partner} be seeing — a worry or a hope — that you're not?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: The future looks different at different distances. Is {partner} looking closer in, or further out, than you are? Write what they might see there.
closing_text: Looking at different distances isn't disagreement. It's two sets of eyes on one road.

text: The everyday machine is running well for both of you. Name one thing that's quietly working, before it turns invisible.
closing_text: The quietly working things run the whole show. Good catch.

text: What invisible work has {partner} been doing that deserves to be visible? Name one thing.
closing_text: Invisible work, once seen, weighs half as much.

text: Smooth weeks usually have a rhythm underneath. What's the rhythm — and what would you protect about it?
closing_text: Rhythms hold when somebody minds them. Now you both are.

text: Daily life came in low. From your side, where does the day pinch — mornings, evenings, the list that never ends? Describe the pinch, not the fix. Skip if you'd rather — it'll keep.
One small trade this week — a task swapped, a chore dropped entirely. What would you offer?
closing_text: One trade is enough for now. The list will still be there, slightly shorter.

text: The invisible work — planning, remembering, noticing — is easy to miscount. From your side, what are you carrying that might not be seen? Just the list, no tallying. Skipping is fine.
Pick one recurring task to make easier this week — automate it, halve it, or share it. Which one?
closing_text: Easier, not perfect. That's enough for now.

text: Some days are fine and still exhausting. Describe what tired looks like for you lately — it's hard to see from inside someone else's day. You can skip this one.
Name one ten-minute pocket this week that could be just yours — and one that could be just theirs.
closing_text: Two small pockets of air. That's enough for now.

text: You're living in the same household and experiencing it differently. What might {partner}'s day actually feel like, hour by hour?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: What load might weigh on {partner} more than it would ever weigh on you?
closing_text: Same house, different weights. Both counts are honest.

text: You both feel good about how you handle friction. What's the move — the thing one of you does that softens it?
closing_text: A named move is a keepable move.

text: Think of the last disagreement that went well. What did {partner} do that helped? Tell them.
closing_text: What helped once will likely help again — especially now that it's been said.

text: Couples who find their way back usually have a signal — a phrase, a touch, a cup of tea. What's yours?
closing_text: If you have a signal, you have a way home. Worth remembering.

text: This is about the hard stuff, so take it slowly. From your side, what happens between you when things go wrong? Just the shape of it — no fixing yet. You can skip this one. It'll keep.
What's one small thing that would make the next disagreement five percent softer — a pause, a code word, a walk first? Offer one.
closing_text: Five percent softer is a real number. That's enough for now.

text: After a rough moment, people reach for repair differently. What do you tend to need first — space, words, touch? Describe yours, not theirs. Skipping is allowed.
Tell each other one thing that reliably helps you come back together after friction. Ten minutes, once this week.
closing_text: Knowing the way back matters more than never leaving. This will come back around.

text: Some things get dropped instead of finished, and they hum in the background. Is something humming for you? Name only that it exists — not the whole case. It's fine to skip; this will keep.
Choose the smallest true piece of one old topic and say just that piece out loud this week. What's the piece?
closing_text: The smallest piece still counts as begun. That's enough for now.

text: You feel differently about how conflict goes between you. What might disagreements feel like from {partner}'s side — before, during, after?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: One of you may recover faster than the other. What might the slower recovery need that the faster one doesn't?
closing_text: Different speeds of coming back, same direction home.

text: Feeling valued landed high for both of you. What's one recent moment you felt genuinely seen? Describe it.
closing_text: Moments like that are load-bearing. Good to know where they are.

text: What does {partner} do that quietly builds your trust — the reliable, unglamorous things? Name one.
closing_text: The unglamorous things were doing the heavy lifting all along.

text: Steady appreciation is usually a habit, not luck. Which habit is doing it — the thank yous, the noticing?
closing_text: Habits fed are habits kept.

text: Feeling valued came in low, and that deserves a gentle approach. From your side, what's been going unnoticed? Describe it without keeping count. You can skip this one. It'll keep.
One specific thank you this week — for a real thing, said plainly. What would you thank them for?
closing_text: Specific beats grand. That's enough for now.

text: Dips in trust are usually small things repeated, not one big thing. From where you sit, what small thing has been repeating? Just describe it. Skipping is fine.
Name one small promise you could make and keep this week. Small enough to be certain.
closing_text: Kept promises add up quietly. That's enough for now.

text: Feeling unappreciated and being unappreciated aren't always the same thing — both are worth describing. What does it look like from your side? No verdicts. You can skip this.
Catch {partner} doing one thing right this week, and say so soon after. What might you watch for?
closing_text: Noticing is a practice. One catch is enough for now.

text: You feel differently valued right now. What might {partner} already be doing that they count as appreciation — and you might be missing?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: What might feeling valued look like to {partner} — and where might they be looking for it?
closing_text: People look for value in different places. Different numbers often just mean different places.

text: You both feel room to be yourselves. What made the number high — time protected, a pursuit supported, space given freely?
closing_text: Room, given freely, tends to bring people back closer.

text: How has {partner} backed the person you're becoming — a class, a habit, a quiet hour defended? Name it.
closing_text: Now it's said. Support like that grows when it's noticed.

text: Couples who grow well trade freedom generously. What's the arrangement that's working? Say it out loud so it lasts.
closing_text: Arrangements said out loud survive busy seasons.

text: This one can feel tangled — needing space and needing each other at once. From your side, where does it feel tight? Describe the tightness, not the fix. You can skip this one. It'll keep.
One small piece of room this week — an hour claimed, or an hour offered. What would yours be?
closing_text: An hour is enough for now. Room grows from there.

text: Sometimes one person's growing reads as distance to the other. From where you sit, which side of that are you on right now? Just describe it. Skipping is allowed.
Name one solo thing you'd like to do this week — and one way you'll share it with each other afterward.
closing_text: Going and returning. That's the whole shape, and it's enough for now.

text: The person you chose keeps changing — that's the deal. What change in yourself have you not quite said out loud? Gently. It's fine to skip; this will keep.
Ask {partner} one question this week about who they're becoming. Write the question here first.
closing_text: A good question is its own kind of room. That's enough for now.

text: You feel differently about the room you each have. What might space — more of it, or less — mean to {partner} right now?
closing_text: Different numbers usually mean different vantage points, not different commitment.

text: What might {partner} be growing toward that they haven't fully said? Write your best guess, held gently.
closing_text: A guess held gently is a way of paying attention. Different numbers, same direction.
